metadata.title: Davide Avagnano — Software Engineer
metadata.description: Davide Avagnano, freelance software engineer. I build and maintain end-to-end production systems with TypeScript, Node.js and React on PostgreSQL.
metadata.ogTagline: End-to-end production systems. TypeScript, Node.js, PostgreSQL.

hero.role: I build and maintain end-to-end production systems with TypeScript, React, Next.js and Node.js on PostgreSQL.
hero.intro: I've contributed to high-volume platforms and built a complete SaaS almost single-handedly. My center of gravity is the backend and architecture: data modeling, type-safe APIs, async workers and security.
hero.location: Naples, Italy — working remotely, open to relocation.

profile.lead: I come from aerospace engineering. Today I design and build systems that ship to production, from the data model to the interface.
profile.p1: I graduated in Aerospace Engineering at Federico II in 2020. I started a master's and dropped it after two exams: I had realised what I actually wanted to do was write software. From 2021 I taught myself: first the fundamentals — Python for data analysis and scripting, algorithms and data structures — then web development with React, Next.js and TypeScript, growing into full-stack.
profile.p2: Since February 2025 I've worked as a freelance software engineer for several clients. I've contributed to high-volume production platforms — marketing intelligence, e-commerce analytics, cross-platform data reconciliation — and, more recently, designed and built a complete multi-tenant SaaS almost single-handedly, from architecture to production.
profile.p3: My center of gravity is the backend and architecture: data modeling, end-to-end type-safe APIs, idempotent async workers, fine-grained authorization and encryption of personal data. I use AI where it brings real value, with an eye on cost — not as a party trick.
profile.p4: What engineering left me is the method: model the problem before writing the code, and treat living documentation and scheduled refactoring as part of the finished work.

now.title: Software Engineer, consulting
now.description: Working full-time for several clients, in teams of 3 to 5 people, alongside CTOs, designers and marketing. In this phase I designed and built a complete multi-tenant SaaS almost on my own, from architecture to production.

freelance.title: First freelance engagement
freelance.description: I start working as a software engineer on a consultancy basis. I contribute to high-volume performance marketing and data analytics platforms, and build security operations tooling.

webDev.title: Self-taught — web development
webDev.description: From studying to the first real projects: frontend with React, Next.js and TypeScript, then the jump to full-stack. A technical foundation built from scratch, outside academia.

foundations.title: Self-taught — fundamentals and Python
foundations.description: First year of the switch: Python for data analysis and scripting (NumPy, pandas, Matplotlib, Seaborn), plus algorithms, data structures and problem-solving.

masters.title: Master's in Aerospace Engineering
masters.description: Federico II, Naples. I started the master's and left it after the first two exams — Advanced Aerospace Structures and Aircraft Aerodynamics — to move towards software. Their reports (FEM, aerodynamics, CFD) are in the Education section.

skills.patternsList: End-to-end type-safe architectures from database to client, multi-source ETL, event-driven systems and async jobs, fine-grained RBAC, encryption of personal data, multi-tenancy, multi-layer caching, internationalization.

projects.intro: Real case studies, grouped by type. Client projects are told in words: they have no public repository or demo.

labels.noPublicLink: Client project: the code and environments are not public.

bookingSaas.title: Booking SaaS
bookingSaas.tagline: Bookings and online presence for appointment-based businesses, built end-to-end.
bookingSaas.context: A platform covering the full operational cycle of an appointment-based business: the public site where customers book on their own, and the admin app for services, opening hours, staff, offers and notifications. At its heart is the availability engine, which computes free slots on the fly. Built for a client in the appointment-booking space.
highlights: The availability engine is the single source of truth: the same function draws the calendar and validates the booking. When two bookings race for the same slot, the database decides — with a partial unique index.
Authorization on two orthogonal axes in AND: RBAC scopes (anti-escalation) and commercial plan features, cached and fail-closed.

Personal data encrypted at rest (AES-256-GCM) with a blind index for lookups. The encryption boundary is guarded by CI and by TypeScript brand types: misusing it is a compile error, not a production incident.
Optimistic concurrency, universal soft-delete and six idempotent scheduled workers.
Multi-channel notifications with an outbox pattern and retries; an AI layer with cost governance (spend caps, usage log, per-model pricing).
bookingSaas.role: Architecture, full-stack development, documentation and deployment — almost entirely on my own.

ecommerceSaas.title: E-commerce analytics SaaS
ecommerceSaas.tagline: A multi-tenant B2B SaaS for e-commerce analytics and operations.
ecommerceSaas.context: It centralizes data from Shopify, couriers, Meta Ads, exchange rates and fuel prices to compute real-time multi-currency net margins. It includes a conversational assistant with a RAG architecture that answers by querying the data.
highlights: End-to-end type-safety from database to client, across 122 procedures.
Multi-node AI assistant: hybrid BM25 + kNN search on Elasticsearch, SQL generation against a read-only database, and widgets described in natural language.
Background processing with queues, distributed locking and retries with backoff.
Composable multi-level permissions; encrypted personal data; Shopify webhooks verified with HMAC.
Advanced PostgreSQL usage: daterange, JSONB, partial unique indexes.
ecommerceSaas.role: Full-stack development of the platform: architecture, AI/RAG system, background processing, RBAC, integrations.

marketingIntelligence.title: Marketing intelligence platform
marketingIntelligence.tagline: Marketing intelligence and lead generation: three applications covering the whole funnel.
marketingIntelligence.context: Three interconnected applications covering campaign creation, landing pages, event collection, call tracking and data reconciliation across platforms. A multi-tenant marketing intelligence SaaS is the brain, a renderer of branded landing pages is the body, and a configuration-distribution daemon is the nervous system. Domain: lead generation in the US market.
highlights: Cross-platform reconciliation: merging ad spend, web conversions and phone conversions into a single truth, with weighted averages and match scoring. It is the heart of the system and the hardest problem in it.
Dashboards updated in real time via PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY, an automation rule engine and bulk campaign management.
Multi-tenant landing page renderer with a quiz and chatbot engine described in JSON: new funnels without writing code.
Multi-layer caching (reverse proxy → Redis → database) and per-domain builds.
Fine-grained RBAC and more than fifteen worker processes in production.
marketingIntelligence.role: Full-stack contribution: frontend, APIs, workers, database schema, integrations, deployment.

adArbitrage.title: Ad arbitrage ecosystem
adArbitrage.tagline: Ad arbitrage: traffic bought from seven ad networks, routed to twenty-seven vertical sites.
adArbitrage.context: A closed loop of ad arbitrage: buying traffic, multi-language vertical sites, monetization through sponsored search feeds and server-to-server conversion tracking. Three projects: an ETL and analytics backend, a multi-site consumer frontend, and a management dashboard.
highlights: Ten config-driven ETL connectors behind a shared abstraction: adding a data source is configuration, not new code.
Config-driven multi-tenancy: twenty-seven domains served from a single codebase.
Server-to-server conversion tracking, with hashing of personal data.
An AI pipeline for content generation and translations into a headless CMS.
In the dashboard, a reusable universal table and URL-driven state.
adArbitrage.role: A multi-year ecosystem built by many hands: I contributed to it.

logManager.title: log-manager
logManager.tagline: Security operations automation: from detection to response.
logManager.context: It automates the detection→response cycle across multi-server web infrastructure: it analyses IDS alerts through Elasticsearch, produces reports of malicious IPs and manages firewall rules on remote servers — blocking, ageing, pruning.
highlights: A multi-stage, protocol-aware IP filtering pipeline.
Streaming reads over large volumes of alerts (Scroll API), without loading everything into memory.
Firewall rule lifecycle with metadata: every block stays traceable and reversible.
Runtime-validated configuration, structured logging, execution as a system service.
logManager.role: Contributed to the tool's development — parts of the system and its integration with the logging stack and production servers.

authApp.title: Authentication App
authApp.tagline: Authentication with NextAuth v5: login, password reset and two-factor verification by email.

dashboard.title: Dashboard Management
dashboard.tagline: Inventory management app with dynamic charts, an Express API and deployment on AWS.

education.intro: Before software there was engineering. A BSc in Aerospace Engineering at Federico II (105/110), then the first master's courses — Advanced Aerospace Structures and Aircraft Aerodynamics — before I moved towards development. The reports from those two exams are below: finite-element structural analysis, airfoil aerodynamics and CFD simulation. The method — model, simulate, verify against theory — is what I still bring to the code today.

labels.documentNote: Original report in Italian (PDF), downloadable below.

saaFem.title: FEM structural analysis
saaFem.tagline: Static and dynamic finite-element analysis of aircraft components, with Nastran and Femap.
saaFem.context: Report for the Advanced Aerospace Structures exam. Finite-element modeling of structural components — from a wing beam to skin panels — with results verified against the theoretical solutions of De Saint-Venant and Roark's formulas.
highlights: Static analysis of a cantilever beam (a wing idealized at the fuselage): BAR and solid CHEXA elements (about 2,000 nodes), with deflection and stresses matching De Saint-Venant theory and a linearity check across two load cases.
Sensitivity analysis on material, section geometry and constraint compliance, replacing the ideal clamp with elastic springs to estimate its limiting stiffness.
Modal analysis of a wing skin panel (CQUAD4 elements): extraction of the first ten natural frequencies (from 27 Hz up), validated against Roark with a 2% error at the central node.
Frequency response (FRF) under distributed load, verifying that the panel does not resonate on the nodal line of the second mode.
Sizing of a tuned dynamic vibration absorber for an ATR 42 turboprop: an auxiliary mass equal to 10% of the fuselage frame cancels the response at the propeller blade-passing frequency.

adaXfoil.title: Airfoil aerodynamics (Xfoil)
adaXfoil.tagline: Full characterization of an airfoil: lift, compressibility, boundary layer, stall and control surfaces.
adaXfoil.context: First exercise of the Aircraft Aerodynamics exam. Analysis of the Boeing 103 airfoil with Xfoil, from the inviscid incompressible field through viscous and compressibility effects.
highlights: Inviscid field: lift curve slope (Cl_α ≈ 6.94 rad⁻¹), zero-lift angle, aerodynamic center and center-of-pressure behavior.
Compressibility effects: lower critical Mach number estimated with the Von Kármán–Tsien correction and Prandtl–Glauert similarity.
Boundary-layer analysis across Reynolds numbers — shape factor, friction, thicknesses, leading-edge laminar bubble and forced transition.
Viscous stall: Cl_max ≈ 1.82 at 18.4° angle of attack, with pre- and post-stall curves.
Effect of flaps and ailerons on the lift curves and stall angles.

adaAnsys.title: Airfoil CFD simulation (Ansys Fluent)
adaAnsys.tagline: Airfoil CFD from incompressible to supersonic flow: shock waves, shock/boundary-layer interaction and buffet.
adaAnsys.context: Second exercise of the Aircraft Aerodynamics exam. 2D CFD simulation of the same airfoil with Ansys Fluent — meshing, solver and k-ω turbulence model — across the full speed range, comparing results against Xfoil.
highlights: Euler (inviscid) simulations from incompressible to transonic and supersonic flow up to Mach 2, with a near-perfect match to Xfoil in the incompressible field.
Viscous RANS simulations with the k-ω turbulence model, from subsonic to supersonic.
Formation and advance of the normal shock wave on the airfoil's upper and lower surfaces, up to the compressibility crisis — the drag spiking in transonic flow.

In transonic flow at high angle of attack: a lambda shock wave with boundary-layer separation downstream and the onset of buffet, an unsteady phenomenon visible in the oscillation of the aerodynamic coefficients.
Unsteady analysis with vortex shedding, and a systematic comparison with the limits of Xfoil's panel method.

contact.title: Let's work together.
contact.intro: Got a product to ship, a system to put back in order or a team to strengthen? Drop me a couple of lines about what you need.
